musicalLanguage.description: Hoagy Carmichael's Georgia on My Mind is a 44-measure piece at 111 BPM, and its harmony data is close to silent: only 1 chord window clears the confidence gate in the entire piece — C major, at measure 1, hedged as inferred harmony — so beyond noting that single labeled color (which functions as the dominant of the stated F major, a plausible opening gesture), this annotation says nothing further about chords, because the data supports nothing further. What the piece does have is an extraordinary density outlier: measure 31 carries 91 total onsets (29 in the right hand, 62 in the left) — far beyond any other measure in the piece, with the next-busiest, measure 29, at 59. Measure 34 is nearly as dense (58 onsets, 52 of them in the right hand alone) and is also where the pattern pass finds right-hand figures transposed by as much as 29 semitones, consistent with a fast, wide-ranging melodic run rather than a repeated fixed shape. A 30-note phrase heard together in both hands at measures 19-22 returns essentially intact roughly 20 measures later, at measures 39-42.
musicalLanguage.structure: The section lens finds only 4 broad regions in this short piece (measures 1-6, 7-15, 16-28, and 29-44), with the final region — home to the extreme density at measures 29, 31, and 34 — carrying the largest novelty score (2.564). The matched phrase at measures 19-22 and 39-42, confirmed by both the pattern pass and the raw repeat detector's near-identical matches at measures 20/40 and 21/41, straddles the boundary between the third and fourth regions, giving the piece's second half a genuine large-scale echo of its first.
keyMoments: Measure 31 carries 91 total onsets (29 right-hand, 62 left-hand) — an extraordinary outlier even against the piece's own busiest neighbors (59 at measure 29, 58 at measures 23 and 34) — teaching that this passage demands a level of two-hand density and control far beyond anything else in the piece.

Only 1 chord window clears the confidence gate in the whole piece — a single C major at measure 1 — so harmony here is best learned by ear against the stated F major rather than from any labeled progression the data can support.

A 30-note phrase heard together in both hands at measures 19-22 returns essentially intact at measures 39-42, roughly 20 measures later — confirmed independently by the raw repeat detector's own near-identical matches at measures 20/40 and 21/41, a genuine large-scale echo worth recognizing as one idea learned twice.

Measure 34's right-hand material shows transposed repetition of a short figure across a span as wide as 29 semitones, nearly two and a half octaves — evidence of a fast, register-spanning melodic run rather than a fixed hand shape repeated in place.
teachingGoals: Build serious two-hand independence and stamina specifically for measure 31, the piece's extraordinary density peak at 91 onsets, nearly double its nearest neighbors.
Practice trusting the ear over the data for harmony here — with only 1 labeled chord window in the whole piece, this is a case where listening has to do the work a confidence-gated progression can't.

Learn the 30-note phrase at measures 19-22 once, recognizing it returns at measures 39-42 — two appearances from one act of learning, confirmed independently by both the pattern pass and the raw repeat detector.

Practice measure 34's wide-ranging right-hand run across its full register span, since the pattern pass shows the same short shape recurring transposed by up to 29 semitones — think in terms of a traveling gesture, not a fixed hand position.
styleTips: Approach measure 31 as its own dedicated technical project — with 91 onsets packed into one measure, this is not something to sight-read at tempo; isolate it and build it up slowly.
Pedal with real restraint through the extreme density at measures 29-34 — this much simultaneous activity in both hands needs clarity, not a wash of sustain, or the effect will collapse into mud.
Listen for the C major color at measure 1 functioning as a dominant pickup into the stated F major, then let your ear carry the rest of the harmony — the data has almost nothing more to offer here.
Try feeling the return of the measures 19-22 phrase at measures 39-42 as a moment of recognition and release after the intervening development, giving it a slightly different color the second time.
